entry.title: Enhancing Filter Visibility in SAP Analytics Cloud Numeric Point Charts
entry.content:
The following method outlines a straightforward approach to display widget-level filters directly on the Numeric Point chart. This technique is highly scalable, ensuring that any applied filters are automatically updated on the widget, providing clear information on the data points.
This method is particularly beneficial when there is a need to display filters based on Generic Dimensions (e.g., Fiscal Year) on the widget, while the Numeric Point chart itself is designed based on the Account Dimension.

By implementing this approach, users can achieve greater clarity and precision in their data visualization efforts.

Solution
Add a Text Box to the Story Page: -

You can add this text box to sit on top of the Numeric point chart, you can place it in any position on the story or drag it on top of the chart, as shown below I have placed the Text Box on top of the 2nd Numeric Point Chart: -

Click on the … button, then click on +Add -> Dynamic Text as shown below: -
 

A dialog box will open called: Insert Dynamic Text. In this box, scroll down and select the required chart that you want to add the Year value to.
Note: You must be careful in identifying the correct Numeric Point chart such that the correct year filter value will be displayed on each respective Numeric Point chart.
Process is shown below: -

1) Here I have selected the 2nd Numeric Point Chart: Operation, because I want to refer to the year value of only this chart.
2) Drill down on the filter section of the chart
3) Select the required filter value, here it is Plan Year.
4) Once you are done with the above, click on Create in Insert Dynamic text box.

The Year value will now start showing on the text box now: -
 
Note: You can also add some text manually (before & after to the year value) if you so require. Just click on the year value and position your cursor as needed.

For formatting the value in the text box, since this is dynamic text, just select the value and then right click the value in the text box and then select Edit Styling… as shown below: -
 

You can make multiple filter selections(e.g., on Year) on the Numeric Point Chart and then based on that the Text Box value will change automatically.

entry.title: Data Templates for Concur Data Delivery Service (DDS) : CREDIT CARD PROGRAM MANAGEMENT SAC Story
entry.content:
Overview

The Concur Expense Data Templates for the Concur Data Delivery Service (DDS) Expense content package offers a powerful solution for visualizing and analyzing data captured in SAP Concur. Seamlessly integrated with SAP Data Delivery Services and utilizing the SAP Analytics Cloud for data visualization, this solution provides administrators with comprehensive insights into organizational expenses, system administration, and user distributions. Its advanced analytics capabilities include robust auditing and control features essential for fraud detection and compliance assurance.

Built upon Analytical Models in SAP Datasphere and visualized in SAP Analytics Cloud, this content package encompasses a suite of detailed stories that deliver in-depth perspectives on expense management. These insights empower administrators to make informed decisions, optimize expense workflows, and enhance overall financial governance.

One standout feature is the Credit Card Program Management SAC Story. Designed to streamline and oversee credit card programs, it integrates and visualizes card usage data to provide a holistic view of cardholder activity and program efficiency. Additionally, it offers detailed insights into credit card transactions, highlighting spending patterns, transaction volumes, and potential anomalies, thereby significantly enhancing financial oversight and fraud detection.

Credit Card Program Management

This dashboard offers a comprehensive overview of your company's financial landscape by presenting key metrics related to credit card spend. It serves as a centralized hub for monitoring and analyzing credit card related financial performance, aiding in strategic decision-making and budget management.  

  • Adoption Rate: Presenting the proportion of credit card usage in total transactions (entries) 
  • Adoption Rate by Employee Location: Analysis of the geographic spread of credit card adoption among employees 
  • Top Spenders by Payment Type: Shows a breakdown of highest credit card spend across Payment Types 
  • Top Spenders by Payment Type: Provides key insights into highest credit card spenders and their payment preferences. 
  • Top Credit Card Spend by Vendor: Displays the vendors with the highest credit card expenditure.  

 

Credit Card Transaction Analysis

This dashboard offers an analytical view of credit card spend across your organization. It provides key metrics related to credit card expenses yet to be processed, allowing to monitor and manage unprocessed spend. By highlighting the distribution of transactions across age brackets or per approval status this dashboard aids tracking credit card spend across time or also allows a view via the regional distribution, thus allowing a complete overview for making decisions.  

  • Aging Card Transactions: This chart shows the distribution of Card Transactions across different age brackets.  
  • Spend by Country: Provides a breakdown of credit card expenditure across countries.  
  • Credit Card Transaction per Approval Status: Displays an analysis of transactions by approval status.  
  • Top Employees with Unprocessed CC Transactions: Presents key employees with outstanding credit card transactions.  
  • Credit Card Transaction Details: Displays a detailed list of employee credit card spend.  

Resources:

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/spend-management-blogs-by-sap/data-templates-for-dds-expense-administration-sac-story/ba-p/13805542

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/spend-management-blogs-by-sap/data-templates-for-concur-data-delivery-expense/ba-p/13799924

  • https://www.concur.com/data-delivery-service

  • https://api.sap.com/package/SAPConcurExpenseDDSIntegrationwithSAPDatasphere/overview

entry.title: SAC Report Broadcasting - Blog 2 - Live Data
entry.content:
Introduction

Hello and welcome to part 2 of this blog series. In this blog I will be discussing the functionality of report broadcasting in SAC when the data is live from a data source. I will address the following points:

  • Supported Live Connections
  • Setting up
  • How to create publications
  • View and Modify your Scheduled Publications
  • Data Validation and Refresh

 

Supported Live Connections

You can schedule publications in SAC for the following live data sources:

  • SAP BW
  • SAP HANA
  • SAP S/4HANA
  • SAP HANA 2.0
  • SAP HANA Cloud (Must be a HANA Cloud connection type)
  • Stories based on live data tunnel connections

Unfortunately, data coming live from SAP Datasphere into SAC cannot be scheduled.

 

Setting up

The first step you need to take to set up SAC report broadcasting for live connections is allowing the tenant to schedule a publication. You can set up the tenant so that SAC can publish to either SAP users or non-SAP users or both. This is the same step for scheduling import data for publication however there are some additional steps that need to be taken.

 

  1. Login to your SAC tenant
  2. Go to System >> Administration. Click on the System Configuration tab and toggle the two schedule publication options on.
  3. Go to the Data Source Configuration tab and scroll down to the ‘Live Data Sources’ section. You need  to toggle the ‘Allow live data to securely leave my network’ on.
  4. Navigate to your Connections app and select and edit your live connection. Open the ‘Advanced Features’ header. Check the box beside ‘Let users set up a schedule for publishing stories’. This will use the credentials of the user who schedules the story publishing to be used to retrieve the data.
  5. You will be asked to select a Location ID as well as a Virtual Host and Port.

6. Once these advanced settings are configured successfully, you can schedule a story created on a live connection.

 

How to create publications

  1. Go to your files in SAC
  2. Select the story or analytical application you want to schedule for publication. Choose the Share option and select Schedule Publication
  3. The Schedule Publication window will open. Here you will add your publication information such as event name, start date etc.

Name

Give your Schedule a name

Start Date

Give your schedule a start date and time. You can add a recurrence so that the publication is scheduled to recur every day/ week/ month etc. You can also add details about ending the recurrence.

File Type

You can choose between either a PDF, PowerPoint or CSV

Email Subject

This is the subject for the email which would be delivered to the recipients

Email Message

This is the message body for the email which would be sent to the recipients over email

Email Delivery Options

You can choose which email delivery option you’d like:

·       Attachment

·       Attachment and Preview Inline

·       Preview Inline

Include link to story

If you select this checkbox, the story/ analytic application link would be sent along with the email.

 

4. Add details to the Distribution heading

SAP Analytics Cloud Recipients

Add SAP users or teams to this view

Non-SAP Analytics Cloud Recipients

Add non-SAP users to this view e.g. joe.bloggs@hotmail.com . You can input a maximum of 3 non-SAC users.

Story view

You can choose the story, global bookmarks or my bookmarks.

File Name

The name the file will appear under in your email as a attachment

 

5. Click on Create. This will set up a schedule a publication.

 

View and Modify your Scheduled Publications

  1. Go to the Calendar app in SAC
  2. You can see in Calendar, your scheduled publication events and its recurrence.
  3. You can also edit you’re a recurrence event. Select the recurrence event from the calendar view and on your right side, a new panel opens where you can modify.

 

 

Data Validation and Refresh

Like our previous blog, it is important for the data in the scheduled publications are up to date an accurate especially if they are recurring. A user does not want to receive the wrong data or the same data that they received in last week’s report. Live data is live meaning it is always up to date and refreshed.

 

Conclusion

SAC Report Broadcasting with live data simplifies the work of delivering up-to-date reports to your audience. By supporting various live data connections and offering flexible scheduling options, it ensures that recipients always receive the most current information. This functionality enhances the accuracy and reliability of your reporting, making it an essential tool for real-time data distribution.

entry.title: SAC Add-in for Excel
entry.content:
Introduction

Hello and welcome to this blog discussing the powerful SAC Add-in tool developed by SAC to allow users to seamlessly integrate SAC with Excel in real-time. In this blog I will discuss the following points:

  • What's going on with Analysis for Office?
  • How to get the add-in
  • What is the SAC add-in for Excel?
  • Configurations and Set up

 

What's going on with Analysis for Office?

After digging through other blogs and exploring multiple websites, I finally have a clear answer to what the current situation is regarding Datasphere and AFO. All new features are now being developed only for the SAP Analytics Cloud add-in for Microsoft Office. As a result, new Datasphere Analytic Models can be used with this add-in, but not with SAP Analysis for Microsoft Office (AFO). SAP has no plans to support these analytical models in SAP Analysis for Microsoft Office.

Analytical datasets can still be view in AFO as of August 2024 but will soon be deprecated.

The long and the short of it is that you should use the Analytics Cloud add-in for Microsoft Office. 

How to get the add-in

From the link below you can download and install the Microsoft add-in for Excel:  

https://appsource.microsoft.com/en-us/product/office/wa200000169?tab=overview 

 

What is the SAC add-in for Excel?

The SAC add-in for Microsoft Excel allows users to seamlessly integrate SAC with Excel in real-time. This add-in enhances SAC’s analytics and planning features by incorporating familiar Microsoft Excel functions and interface. This add-in lets users’ format, analyse, and enhance their data using the latest content from the SAC platform including content coming from Datasphere. It is also possible to add one or several tables, each based on one model, to a workbook, add or remove dimensions and measures to analyse data, create a filter to specify your analysis, add totals in rows and columns, drill into hierarchies as needed.

 

Configurations and Set up

1. Open Excel and go to SAP Analytic Cloud tab

 

2. Click on 'Sign In' and it will open a window on the right side. Paste in your SAC URL and click go

 

3. Sign in using your SAC login details

 

4. Click on ‘Add Data Source for Formulas’ and you’ll get a pop-up window like this. You’ll get a list of data sources available from/through SAC. For this example, I will connect to SAP Datasphere.

 

5. Once you click on SAP Datasphere, you’ll be asked to log into Datasphere.

6. Once you sign into Datasphere, you’ll see a list of all your spaces within Datasphere

 

7. Once you select your Space (for this example → Test Space), you can then select your view (for this example → Sales_Orders_View_GEM)

 

8. You can then create your table by adding dimension and/or measures from the builder on the right hand side.

 

Conclusion

The SAC Add-in for Excel provides an easy-to-use and powerful way to integrate SAC into your Microsoft Office workflow. By enhancing Excel’s analytic capabilities and ensuring real-time data updates, this add-in empowers users to work more efficiently with the familiar Office interface.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud, embedded edition – the Way Ahead
entry.content:
Today, SAP Analytics Cloud, embedded edition is available in the Cloud Platform, enterprise agreement environment (CPEA).   

How is SAP Analytics Cloud, public edition different from SAP Analytics Cloud, embedded edition? 

The current service for SAP Analytics Cloud, embedded edition has limited BI functionality and is priced per tenant.  It is limited to one HANA Cloud data source and 150 simultaneous users.  The full-use services for SAP Analytics Cloud, public edition will include full BI and Planning functionality and be priced on a user metric, just as the subscription version of SAP Analytics Cloud.  The same minimums will be maintained between CPEA(Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement) and subscription.  Functionally, it will be the exact same as SAP Analytics Cloud public edition sold as subscription.   

 What is the Process for Technical Migration? 

 At any time, a customer can start the new full-use service and use Analytics Content Network to migrate their content from SAC, embedded edition tenants to CPEA SAC, public edition tenants.   You will need to start the SAC, public edition service first, in order to import the content. 

 What Happens to the Existing Customers? 

 During your CPEA contract duration, you’re still able to access the SAP Analytics Cloud, embedded edition in the SAP BTP cockpit. After your contract ends, the SAP Analytics Cloud, embedded edition instance will be removed from your sub-account and you won’t be able to provision new service instances. Additionally, you won't be able to access your existing applications, create new ones, nor use the export functionality.

entry.title: SAP Datasphere Security & Data Access Controls Overview
entry.content:
This article and video are ideal for any system administrator or security architect to address common questions to ensure a successful implementation. Focusing on key items you should know sooner rather than later to avoid surprises. For example:

  • How user security works and the use of Scoped Roles with associated best practices
  • Why mapping on e-mail for SAML SSO could create users with inconsistent userIDs between SAP Analytics Cloud and Datasphere (it may not matter, but worth knowing why)
  • Why dynamic user creation when SAML SSO is mapped on e-mail could be problematic for Analytics Cloud, unlike Datasphere.
  • What are the benefits of a predictable userID in SAP Analytics Cloud unlike the same need in Datasphere.
  • How SAML SSO impacts Data Access Controls.
  • Overview of Data Access Controls and demonstrations of implementing each of the 3 structure options
  • How Spaces in Datasphere can be used with Data Access Controls to ensure users only see the data they should.
  • Finally, additional best practices

Feel free to post a comment, but if you'd like a reply please instead post a question. (Since only 'questions' enable replies and keep the context of the question)

 

Resources

 

Preview and presentation download (version 1.0.1)
Contents copied below into this blog post for easier access

Preview and download as Microsoft PowerPoint PPTx
Video - 1 hour 25 mins (version 1.0)

Video bookmarks

  • Application users
    • User access is granted by roles
    • What this means
    • Best Practice (likely to evolve over time)
    • Separate user directories (includes why dynamic user creation with SAML SSO mapped on e-mail could be problematic)
    • For SAP Analytics Cloud, there are benefits of a predictable USERID of your choice
    • Best Practice
  • Database Users
    • Demo of creating a new scoped role following best practice, assigning a user to a space, creating a view and consuming it as a database user
  • Overview row-level security
    • Concept
    • Structures with ‘Single Values’
      • Demo of creating Data Access Control with ‘Single Values’
    • Structures with ‘Operator and Values’
      • Demo of creating Data Access Control with ‘Operator and Values’
    • Structures with ‘Hierarchy’
      • Demo of creating Data Access Control with ‘Hierarchy’
  • Securing Data Access Controls with the use of ‘Spaces’
  • Additional practical and best practice advice

 

Application users

User access is granted by roles:

  • Global Roles:
    • Global roles grant privileges for Service (tenant) administration
    • The union of all global roles determines the privileges
    • Typically administrators need global roles, unlike regular users

  • Scoped Roles:
    • Grants both privilege and access to ‘Spaces’
    • Membership of the Space grants access to artefacts stored within, such as tables, views etc.
    • Users assigned to a particular space have access to all artefacts and data within that space
    • Access to a Space can only be granted via a ‘Scoped Role’, not a global role
    • Scoped Roles can not be set as ‘default’, which means new users are not automatically assigned a scoped role (see next slide for options)

 What this means

  • Granting access requires a single-scoped role since it grants both application privileges and access to Space(s)
  • The same user can have different rights in different Spaces
    • User could be a ‘viewer’ of one Space and a ‘modeller’ of another

Best Practice (likely to evolve over time)

  • Consider limiting re-using scoped roles for multiple spaces, especially if manually creating users and assigning them to Spaces
    • It isn’t always obvious when granting a scoped role to a user which Spaces they have access to
    • Use of SAML mapping for roles will add all spaces of the scoped role
    • The workflow to add roles to a user (Menu-Security-Users) will add all spaces of the scoped role
    • Adding a new Space to an existing scoped role, will add all existing users of that scoped role to the new Space
    • The following enables you to specify which Spaces of a scoped role are granted:
      • Command line interface (doc)
      • SCIM API (now available, however, SAP Provisioning Service support is planned later. Adding a role via SCIM adds all spaces of the scoped role)
      • Workflow to add users to a Space (Menu-Space Management)
      • Workflow to add users to roles (Menu-Security-Roles)

 

  • Avoid using standard application roles, instead use them as a template for custom global roles and scoped roles
    • Only the rights of template ‘Scoped role’ can be edited if those templates are ‘custom’ roles, not standard roles
    • SAP will update the standard application roles over time, which means you’re not in complete control when updates are applied. Always use a custom role instead.

 

Separate user directories

  • User accounts are held in each application, SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud
    • Each application has its own roles and authorization model
    • Means a user is created and maintained in each application

 

  • For both applications: When using SAML SSO and ‘dynamic user creation’ is enabled, the ‘user ID’ assigned depends on the SAML SSO attribute mapping:
  • When mapping on USERID, the USERID used
  • When mapping on email, the USERID is derived from the email address!
    • MATTHEW, would be the USERID for the email matthew@Sisn.com

 

For SAP Analytics Cloud, there are benefits of a predictable USERID of your choice:

  • Users make sense of the SAC USERID, it appears within the user interface
  • Easier to control, row-level security (for SAC-acquired models) is based on SAC USERID
  • Gain insights easier as the activities log identifies the user by SAC USERID
  • Resolves sharing permissions when content moves about the landscape as some artefacts are stored within themselves who can access them by SAC USERID

For Datasphere

  • The ‘identifier’ used by Datasphere Data Access Control, is the attribute you map users upon
    • If mapping on ‘email’, Datasphere row-level access will be authorized based on ‘email’ unlike SAP Analytics Cloud which is always by SAC USERID

 

Best Practice

  • Map on USERID if possible (even when users may identify themselves by email)
    • Guaranteed to get a known USERID which is ideal for SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Can map on a different attribute for each application, but not great for consistency
  • The use of SCIM API resolves this problem regardless of the SAML SSO attribute mapping, i.e. even when email is used, the user ID can be fully controlled by you
    • Scimv2 is needed to resolve this problem, not possible with scimv1 (for both SAC and Datasphere)

Database users

 

Database only users

  • Users can also be created for ‘database’ only access
  • Such users can read and/or write based on database-level privileges
  • Each database user has an Open SQL schema automatically created for them
  • Ideal for 3rd party tools that require only view-level access

 

  • Such users can not enjoy Business Layer objects such as Analytical Models
  • SAP Analytics Cloud users must connect to SAP Datasphere as regular Application users (i.e. not database-only users)

 

Overview row-level security

Data Access Controls Concept

Application of row-level security requires:

  • 1) A ‘Data Access Control’ object (red outlined above)
    • Such objects contain a ‘Permissions Entity’ which is a table/view
    • 1 of 3 different table ‘structures’ must be specified
      • ‘single values’ (as shown)
      • ‘operator and values’
      • or, ‘hierarchy’
  • 2) Joining the ‘Data Access Control’ object to a table/view
    • The ‘permission entities’ are joined to the ‘data’ table thus reducing the rows accordingly – see 2 for ‘single values’

 

  • The data is then filtered by the user accessing the ‘data’ table
  • User identifier is always the same as the ‘Subject NameID’ used to map the user to Datasphere as part of the SAML Single-Sign-On
    • This can be a USERID, email, or a custom property
    • For the default Authentication method, i.e. without any custom SAML SSO, the identifier is the email
  • More details on ‘operator and values’ and ‘hierarchy’ structures next

 

Structures with ‘Single Values’

 Structures with ‘Single Values’

 

  • Row per value
    • For 100 values, you’d need 100 rows
  • Each entity is an ‘AND’ with others

 

  • Ideal for simple use cases
    • not necessarily the best choice for performance when lots of values are needed
    • Documentation

 

Structures with ‘Operator and Values’

Structures with ‘Operator and Values’:

  • Support much more complex logic
  • Requires a more complex ‘permission table’ to support operators, restriction, criterion, operator, first and optional second values
  • Support operators:
    • ALL (or *) - Provides access to all records.
    • N (or IS NULL) - Is null.
    • NN (or NOT NULL) - Is not null.
    • EQ (or = ) - Equal to First Value.
    • NE (or <> or !=) - Not equal to First Value.
    • GT (or >) - Greater than First Value.
    • GE (or >=) - Greater than or equal to First Value.
    • LT (or <) - Less than First Value.
    • LE (or <=) - Less than or equal to First Value.
    • CP (or LIKE) - Contains pattern First Value.
    • BT (or BETWEEN) - Between First Value and Second Value.
  • Documentation

 

Operator and Values

  • Based on these three records:
  • bob will have access to records with a:
    • (restriction 0) - Code between CA and CZ, and having a Type equal to 1,
    • or
    • (restriction 1) - Class beginning with ERR.
  • ann will have access to all records:
    • (restriction 0) thanks to the ALL operator.

 

  • The restriction defines the ‘OR’ and ‘AND’ operations:
    • Restrictions of the same value are AND’ed
    • Restrictions of different values are OR’ed

Based on these three records:

 

  • MSHAW will have access to records with a:
    • (restriction 0) - Code between CA and CZ, and having a Type equal to 1,
    • or
    • (restriction 1) - Class contains a pattern with ERR.

 

Structures with ‘Hierarchy’

Structures with ‘Hierarchy’:

  • The entity must have a semantic usage of ‘hierarchy’
  • Only external hierarchies with a single pair of parent-child columns are supported
  • Not supported:
    • Level-based hierarchies,
    • dimensions with internal hierarchies,
    • and entities with a semantic usage of Hierarchy with Directory
  • Hierarchy table must have:
    • A single root node with a parent value of null
    • No nodes with multiple parents
    • No circular relationships
  • Documentation

 

Hierarchy example

  • Based on these three records:
    • bob will have access to: Europe, France, or Paris
    • jim will have access to: France or Paris
    • ann will have access to: Paris

 

Securing Data Access Controls With the use of ‘Spaces'

 

Spaces enable Data Access Controls to be secured

  • ‘Permissions’ Space:
    • Only users with access can edit the permissions
  • ‘IT’ Space:
    • Data Access Controls can’t be defined on a shared table directly, so a view is needed
    • Users re-use the Permissions table (read-only) to define Data Access Control upon,
      …and define the joins with the data view, which is then shared
  • ‘Sales’ Space:
    • Users re-use the ‘Sales’ view (read-only) which makes it impossible to avoid the enforced Data Access Controls defined on the data
  • For more details see the official documentation

 

Additional practical and best practice advice

Consider the use of virtual tables for ‘Permissions’

  • Use virtual tables, instead of physical tables where necessary to reduce duplication
  • However, enable replication for source tables, particularly if any source contains more than 500,000 rows (doc)

Restrictions to restrictions!

  • Data Access Controls cannot be applied on views with input parameters
    • (limitation planned to be removed later)
  • View persistency cannot be performed on views protected by Data Access Controls with ‘Operator and Values’ structures
  • SQL Script within the view enables additional flexibility if needed:
    • Example blogs using hierarchies and wildcards, now redundant since they are supported natively

Re-using authorizations

  • Importing Remote Authorization from SAP BW and BW/4HANA is supported
    • (see blog post)

entry.title: Data Templates for Concur Data Delivery Service (DDS) : POLICY COMPLIANCE SAC Story
entry.content:
Overview

The Data Templates for the Concur Data Delivery Service (DDS) Expense content package offers a powerful solution for visualizing and analyzing data captured in SAP Concur Expense. Seamlessly integrated with SAP Data Delivery Services and utilizing the SAP Analytics Cloud for data visualization, this solution provides administrators with comprehensive insights into organizational expenses, system administration, and user distributions. Its advanced analytics capabilities include robust auditing and control features essential for fraud detection and compliance assurance.

Built upon Analytical Models in SAP Datasphere and visualized in SAP Analytics Cloud, this content package encompasses a suite of detailed stories that deliver in-depth perspectives on expense management. These insights empower administrators to make informed decisions, optimize expense workflows, and enhance overall financial governance.

The Policy Compliance narrative centers on spotting and handling expense anomalies to maintain adherence to policies. Utilizing visual tools and insights, it facilitates the early detection and resolution of policy breaches, thereby strengthening financial governance and compliance efforts.

 

Policy Compliance

This dashboard provides a detailed summary of your users' adherence to company policies by monitoring expense exceptions. It enables you to assess whether users and approvers correctly understand and follow the policy. By examining various metrics, you can implement corrective actions, such as reaching out to users or adjusting the policy as needed. An input control feature allows you to toggle between exceptions related to entire claims or individual expenses.

  • Number of Exceptions: Total of Exceptions attached to expenses.
  • Exception Volume / Amount Over Time: Revealing the evolution of exceptions over time.
  • Top Policy Exceptions: Providing a top exceptions analysis, in term of amount, occurrences and number of employees.
  • Top Employees with Highest No of Exceptions: identifying users with the highest number of exceptions.
  • Top Expenses with Highest No of Exceptions: Providing a ranking of the Expense Types with most exceptions attached to them.
  • Number of Exceptions by Approval Status: displaying the exceptions’ repartition by Expense Reports statuses.
  • Exceptions details: The exhaustive list and breakdown of all occurrences.

Resources:

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/spend-management-blogs-by-sap/data-templates-for-dds-expense-administra...
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/spend-management-blogs-by-sap/data-templates-for-concur-data-delivery-e...
  • https://www.concur.com/data-delivery-service
  • https://api.sap.com/package/SAPConcurExpenseDDSIntegrationwithSAPDatasphere/overview

entry.title: Exploring Data Access Control: Ensuring Consistency from SAC to Datasphere and Back - Blog 2
entry.content:
Introduction

Welcome to the next instalment of this blog series. In this post, I will address the question: “Will DAC settings be preserved when sending data from SAC back to Datasphere?” Just to recap, in the previous blog we spoke about the following:

  • What is Data Access Control (DAC) in SAC
  • The different type of DACs – dimension-based and role based
  • Benefits & drawbacks for both dimension-based and role-based controls
  • Which Type of Data Access Control Is Best for Different Situations

 

Throughout this blog, I will discuss the following in relation to data access control coming from SAC to Datasphere:

  • Why would you send data from SAC to Datasphere?
  • Scenario 1 - will DACs be preserved when sending data from SAC back to Datasphere?
  • Scenario 2 - will DACs be preserved when sending data from Datasphere to SAC?

 

Why would you send data from SAC to Datasphere?

Typically, we consider Datasphere as the backend and SAC as the frontend for reporting. However, in this scenario, SAC is used as the backend to store planning data in a planning model. We then aim to send this data from SAC to Datasphere. But why? Here are several reasons:

  • Advanced Data Processing and Transformation - Datasphere provides advanced data transformation capabilities. By sending data from SAC, users can use Datasphere's tools for complex data processing that might not be possible directly within SAC. You can also enrich data with additional information from other sources stored in Datasphere. This can enhance the value, and insights derived from SAC data.

 

  • Enhanced Analytical Capabilities - Datasphere offers advanced analytics tools that can be used to analyse planning data in more depth. This includes predictive analytics and machine learning capabilities that can provide more insightful planning forecasts.

 

  • Scalability and Performance - Datasphere is designed to handle large volumes of data efficiently, ensuring that data can be processed and analysed without performance bottlenecks. Offloading heavy data processing tasks to Datasphere can improve the performance of SAC, ensuring that both planning, and analytics tasks are carried out efficiently.

 

Let’s consider 2 scenarios:

  1. DACs are applied in SAC for User 1 to only see the Soft Drinks data – will user 1 be able to only see the Soft Drinks data when they log into Datasphere?
  2. DACs are applied in Datasphere for User 1 to only see the Soft Drinks data - will user 1 be able to only see the Soft Drinks data when they log into SAC?

 

Scenario 1

In the first scenario, we aim to confirm that DAC settings applied to user 1 within SAC do not carry over when the data from SAC is imported to Datasphere for the same user. Specifically, within the SAC tenant, a manager will assign DAC to user 1, restricting their view to only Soft Drinks data in SAC. Since the DACs are managed within SAC, they will not be enforced in Datasphere when previewing the imported data, as Datasphere does not directly handle the data with these DAC configurations.

 

Steps

  1. Setting up DACs in SAC & setting up the connection
  2. Import the fact data
  3. Result

 

1. Setting up DACs in SAC & setting up the connection

Setting up DAC in SAC has been discussed here in part 1 of this blog series. The detailed steps for setting up a connection between the two tenants can be found here. For a quick overview, refer to the following steps:

  1. Create an OAuth Client in SAC: Make sure to note the relevant information.
  2. Create a CDI Connection to SAC in Datasphere: Use the information noted in the previous step to complete this process.

 

2. Import the fact data

Once the connection is created, I imported the fact data from a remote table in Datasphere from SAC. Once imported, you can add a graphical view on top of this remote table.

 

3. Result

We can then preview the data from this view. To our surprise, my user can see all drink departments. This shows that the DACs that were created in SAC have not followed through to Datasphere. You can see below all drink departments coming through from SAC.

 

 

Scenario 2

In the second scenario, we want to verify that DACs applied within the Datasphere tenant are still enforced when the user accesses the data through SAC. Specifically, when a manager assigns a DAC to user 1, restricting their view to only Soft Drinks data in Datasphere, this restriction should persist when the user accesses the same data in SAC. The SAC story will access the data directly from Datasphere, bypassing any internal SAC models. Therefore, the DACs configured in Datasphere will remain effective, ensuring that user 1 can still only view the Soft Drinks data within SAC.

 

Steps

  1. Setting up DACs in Datasphere & setting up the connection
  2. Import Actual Data from Datasphere via an OData Connection
  3. Result

 

1. Setting up DACs in Datasphere & setting up the connection

Setting up DAC in Datasphere has been discussed here. For this example, In Datasphere, we assigned DACs to user 1 so that they can only view the Soft Drinks data. See data preview below.

 

The detailed steps for setting up a connection between the two tenants can be found here & here. For a quick overview, refer to the following steps:

  1. Create an OAuth Client in Datasphere: Make sure to note the relevant information.
  2. Create an OData Connection to Datasphere in SAC: Use the information noted in the previous step to complete this process.

 

2. Import Actual Data from Datasphere via an OData Connection

We have discussed the steps to import the actual data from Datasphere via an OData connection here. Just to give a quick summary you must first start by creating a new model in the Modeler and select "Start with data." Choose "OData Services" and select the connection you previously created. Create a new query, add the necessary query details, and select the desired table with up to 100 columns. After importing the data, you can clean and finalize your model. Finally, create a story to test and ensure everything is functioning correctly.

 

3. Result

When user 1 accesses the story within SAC, the DACs will maintain its effectiveness. This means that user 1 can only see the data related to the Soft Drinks department. This data is stored in Datasphere, the Data Access Controls (DAC) are, therefore, managed by Datasphere. The story in SAC accesses this data directly from Datasphere, not from an internal SAC model. Therefore, any DAC configured in Datasphere remains effective when the data is viewed through an SAC story.

 

 

See the diagram below to see how the DACs work between the SAC and Datasphere tenants.

 

 

Conclusion

In this final part of our series, we explored whether Data Access Controls (DAC) follow through when sending data from SAC back to Datasphere. We examined the setup of DAC in SAC and discussed the steps for establishing a connection between SAC and Datasphere. Our findings reveal that when data is stored in Datasphere, DACs are managed by Datasphere and remain effective when accessed via SAC. However, if data from an SAC model is imported into Datasphere, the DACs configured in SAC do not apply in Datasphere, as the data is not accessed directly within SAC. Understanding these distinctions is key to managing data effectively across platforms.

entry.title: Gen AI meets SAP: Improve your sales strategies with SAP S/4HANA, SAP Datasphere and SAC
entry.content:
Introduction

In my previous blog , How Metadata Enrichment can enhance SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine on GenAI scenarios , i focused on leveraging the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine together with Metadata Enrichment through GenAI, to further extend analytical insights. This blog will focus on combining sales data from SAP S/4HANA with product reviews, centrally stored in SAP Datasphere. Leveraging the powerful analytics capabilities of SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), we are will generate actionable sales insights that uplift the conventional sales strategies.

This blog  will explore the benefits of combing sentiment analysis (using SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine and Large Language Models [LLM])  and SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). These cutting-edge technologies together enhance sales and market strategies. These tools, can help businesses transition from basic descriptive analyses to advanced predictive and prescriptive insights, thereby unlocking  significant market opportunities. We'll examine how sentiment analysis reveals customer sentiment from product reviews, while LLMs and SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine facilitate the processing and contextualization of vast data sets. Integration solutions,part of SAP BTP, ensure seamless data flow and scalability, as well as real-time responsiveness. 

Integrating Sales Data with Product Review Sentiments

The first step is to integrate sales data from SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere. At the same time, we will take advantage of Generative AI (Gen AI) to analyze product reviews, applying sentiment analysis to understand customer opinions and trends. These reviews, enriched with sentiment scores and categorized against segments of interest (such as product, supplier or delivery experience), are then ingested into SAP Datasphere. This approach harmonizes structured and unstructured data; and also prepares the data for advanced, multi-dimensional analytics.

By combining transactional data with customer sentiments, businesses can gain valuable insights using SAP Analytics Cloud. This integration allows companies to use years of logged data to identify areas for improvement such as enhancing customer support and optimizing delivery services. This data-driven approach helps businesses shift from reactive decision-making to proactive strategies that meet market needs and improve customer satisfaction.

Leveraging Generative AI for Sentiment Analysis of Product Reviews ( Using Synthetic Data )

To conduct this analysis, we used an approach similar to the one in the previous blog. However, in this study, we created synthetic data that closely matches the characteristics of the original dataset. Using synthetic data is important because it allows us to test and train our models without compromising the privacy and integrity of the actual data. Nowadays, generating synthetic data is quite simple with generative AI. In order to do this, each product review was converted into a vector on SAP HANA Cloud within SAP Datasphere. We enriched these vectors with the sentiments and information found in the original dataset.

We used Gen AI to:

  • Create reviews that have the same overall sentiment as the original ones.
  • Mention similar issues encountered, such as product delivery and price sensitivity.
  • Address other relevant factors by considering them at different hierarchical levels.

This approach ensures that our synthetic data is both secure and highly functional for analysis

Synthetic dataset regarding product reviews:

  • User's Overall Review (negative, positive, neutral)
  • Delivery Review (On Time, Delayed, Never Delivered ...) 
  • Price Review (Value for Money, Overpriced ...)
  • Supplier Review (Satisfied, Very Disappointed ...)
  • Customer Support (Satisfied, Very Disappointed ...) 
  • Issues Encountered (Breakouts, Missing Brush ...)
  • Issues Resolutions (Price decrease, Product Refund ...)
  • Resolution Level 1 (Delivery, Packaging ...)
  • Resolution Level 2 (Accessories, Leakage ...)
  • Resolution Level 3 (Color Difference, Expiration Date ...)

    The information concerning the issues of resolutions and their resolving levels was too extensive. An additional pass through using GenAI was necessary to organize this data in a more meaningful manner. Additionally, four more summarized pieces of information regarding the reviews were added :

    • Resolution (Improved Packaging, Improved Pricing ...)
    • Level 1 (Customer Support, Safety Concerns ...)
    • Level 2 (Design Issues, Missing Product ...)
    • Level 3 (Broken Parts, Product Quantity ...)

For example, analyzing two product reviews, the following metadata has been extracted:

 

Supplementary Data Sources and Final Integration

Taking into consideration that this is a demo, we limited the available attributes per "Product", "Customer" and "Supplier" entities. The same constraint apply to the Sales Value and Sales Volume metric. By merging sales data from SAP S/4HANA with sentiment analysis within SAP Datasphere, we developed the final analytical model which will be consumed on SAP Analytics Cloud.

The lineage analysis from the SAP Datasphere model is the following:

This model includes the following dimensions: Product, Calendar, Customer and Supplier along with Sales Data.

Transforming Sales Strategy with SAP Analytics Cloud

By leveraging SAC’s advanced analytical capabilities, businesses can now perform multi-faceted analyses to discover hidden patterns and trends on top of the diversified Data Sources.

Sales teams can drill down into regional customer satisfaction and correlate it with supplier efficiency to identify market-specific strategies. Additionally, predictive analytics powered by SAC can forecast future sales trends by analyzing historical data and enriched generative AI insights, allowing for proactive inventory management and targeted marketing campaigns.Moreover, Reviews outlining customer issues may yield insights for further improvement, e.g. improvement in logistics and delivery services.

The synergy between SAC and Gen AI thus transforms static sales reports into a living strategy dashboard. This powerful combination enables a more informed, responsive, and strategic sales approach, strengthening the business’s competitive position in the market.

Introducing the GenAI-Powered SAP Analytic Cloud Dashboard

    1. Sales Overview

This dashboard offers a comprehensive overview of sales data, which can be utilized by different personas within a business to make well-informed decisions. Here are some of the business scenarios, which highlight how various roles can leverage these graphs.

Scenario 1: Sales Performance Optimization

Persona: Sales manager

Objective: Monitor and enhance sales performance.

Analytical Insights:

  • Total sales value, volume, and products: The sales manager can quickly assess the overall sales performance by looking at the total sales value (260,000.00), total volume (3,871), and total products (1,810). This provides a snapshot of the business health.
  • Sales per category and subcategory: By analyzing the sales per category and subcategory, the manager can identify which product lines are performing well and which are underperforming. For instance, "Ready and Cream" appears to be a significant contributor.
  • Top and bottom performing products: This helps in identifying the best-selling products like "Skin Renew" and the least performing ones like "Skin Prep." The manager can then strategize to promote the bottom products or phase them out.

Scenario 2: Customer Segmentation and Targeting

Persona: Marketing team

Objective: Tailor marketing campaigns based on customer demographics.

Actionable Insights:

  • Average sales per customer age group: The marketing team can use this data to understand which age groups are contributing most to sales. For example, the age group 35-44 has the highest average sales. Gen AI can create personalized marketing messages and campaigns targeting specific age groups based on their purchasing behavior. For instance, if Gen AI identifies that the 35-44 age group prefers "Skin Renew," targeted ads can be created for this demographic.
  • Total Sales and Volume per Year/Month: This helps in identifying seasonal trends and planning marketing campaigns accordingly.

Scenario 3: Supplier Relationship Management

Persona: Supply chain manager

Objective: Manage and optimize supplier relationships.

Actionable Insights:

  • Total suppliers and average supplier sentiment per category: The supply chain manager can monitor the performance and sentiment of suppliers. For instance, categories like "Bodycare" and "Skincare" have high sentiment scores, indicating good supplier relationships.
  • Sales per product subcategory: This helps in understanding which subcategories are driving sales and ensuring that suppliers for these subcategories are managed effectively. Gen AI can automate communication with suppliers, ensuring timely updates and addressing any issues promptly. This can improve efficiency and maintain strong supplier relationships.

Scenario 4: Product Development and Innovation

Persona: Product development team

Objective: Innovate and develop new products based on market demand.

Actionable Insights:

  • Top and bottom performing products: By analyzing the top-performing products, the product development team can identify features and attributes that are popular among customers.
  • Sales per category and subcategory: This helps in identifying market trends and gaps, guiding the development of new products that meet customer needs.

Scenario 5: Customer Relationship Management

Persona: Customer relationship manager

Objective: Enhance customer satisfaction and loyalty.

Actionable Insights:

  • Total customers and average sales per customer age group: The customer relationship manager can identify key customer segments and tailor engagement strategies accordingly.
  • Sales per product subcategory: Understanding which products are popular among different customer segments can drive personalized customer interactions and recommendations.

    2. Sales / Sentiment Analysis

 

This dashboard provides a detailed overview of sales and sentiment analysis, segmented by various categories, subcategories, suppliers, and time periods. With user-friendly filters and input controls, it allows users to dive deep into the data based on specific categories, subcategories, suppliers, and time frames. Sentiment analysis is visualized through pie charts that represent customer feelings on price, support, delivery, product, and supplier.

Scenario 1: Product Management and Marketing

Persona: Product or marketing manager

Objective: Identify which products are performing well and which are not. For instance, the "Facecare" product in the "Skin Renew" subcategory has the highest sales ($9,195.00) and a high product rating (4.5). This indicates a successful product that could be further promoted.

Scenario 2: Customer Support Improvement

Persona: Customer support manager

Objective: Sentiment analysis identifies areas that need improvement. The "Customer Support Sentiment" pie chart shows that 46.20% of customers have a negative or very negative sentiment towards support. This is a critical area for improvement in support.

Scenario 3: Supplier Relationship Management

Persona: Supply chain manager

Objective: "Customer Supplier Sentiment" data can be used to evaluate supplier performance. With 52.93% of customers satisfied or very satisfied with suppliers, there is room for improvement.

Scenario 4: Strategic Planning and Forecasting

Persona: Business analyst, strategic planner

Objective: Use the sales trends over time to forecast future sales and plan inventory accordingly. The line graph shows fluctuating sales with peaks around specific dates, indicating potential seasonal trends.

Actionable Insights:

  • Product Rating and Sales: The bar chart shows that "Facecare" and "Shampoo & Conditioner" have high sales and ratings, indicating strong market performance. on the contrary, "Anti-Aging" has lower sales, suggesting a need for marketing efforts or product improvement.
  • Customer Sentiments: The pie charts reveal that while price and delivery sentiments are largely positive, customer support and product sentiments have significant negative feedback. This highlights areas for immediate attention.
  • Product Reviews: The bar chart indicates that "Beauty Tools & Devices" and "Skincare" have the highest volume of reviews, both positive and negative. This suggests these categories are highly engaging but may also have quality issues that need addressing.
  • Customer Demographics: The table shows that the 45-55 age group has the highest sales volume ($75,149.00) and generally positive ratings across all metrics. This demographic could be targeted for loyalty programs or personalized marketing campaigns.

    3. Sales and Sentiment Geo Analysis Overview

This dashboard provides analytical insights, including customer support ratings, price ratings, delivery ratings, supplier ratings, a word cloud of top keywords from product reviews, and a geographical sales distribution map.

Scenario 1: Customer Experience Enhancement

Persona: Customer experience manager

Objective: The customer experience manager can use the dashboard to monitor and improve customer satisfaction.

Actionable Insights:

  • If the delivery rating is low, the manager can investigate logistics and supply chain issues.
  • If the price rating is low, they can consider revising pricing strategies or offering promotions.
  • By analyzing the customer support rating, price rating, delivery rating, and supplier rating, they can identify areas needing improvement.

Scenario 2. Market Expansion Strategy

Persona: Marketing manager

Objective: Use the geographical sales distribution map to identify regions with high and low sales performance.

Actionable Insights:

  • If sales are high in Northern Europe but low in Southern Europe, the manager can allocate more marketing resources to Southern Europe and tailor campaigns to local tastes.
  • Use the word cloud to identify popular product features and emphasize them in marketing materials.

Scenario 3: Product Development and Improvement

Persona: Product manager

Objective: Use the word cloud and customer ratings to gather feedback on product performance and identify areas for improvement.

Actionable Insights:

  • If customers frequently mention "razor" and "blade" in a positive context, the manager can consider expanding the product line to include more shaving products.
  • If "smell" and "oil" are mentioned negatively, they can work on improving the product formulation.
  • if "skin" and "hair" are frequently mentioned, the manager can focus on these aspects in product development.

Scenario 4. Supply Chain Optimization

Persona: Supply chain manager

Objective: Use the delivery rating and geographical sales data to optimize logistics and supply chain operations.

Actionable Insights:

  • If delivery ratings are low in certain regions, the manager can investigate and address bottlenecks in the supply chain.
  • Use sales data to ensure that high-demand regions are adequately stocked.

    4. Detailed Analysis on Issues

Users can explore the complex relationships between product reviews and their corresponding resolution outcomes, segmented by resolution levels ranging from 1 to 3. This tab offers a thorough analysis of how effectively customer issues and reviews have been addressed, providing a detailed view of resolution efficacy. By filtering data based on specific suppliers or products, users can identify patterns and correlations that may affect customer satisfaction and product performance.

Scenario 1: Supplier Performance Analysis

Persona: Supply chain manager

Objective: Identify and address supplier-related issues to improve overall supply chain efficiency.

Actionable Insights:

  • Bar Chart Analysis: The "Nbr of Resolution per Supplier (Top 10)" chart shows that "GlamGlow Goods Delivery" has the highest number of resolutions (287), followed by "PoshParcel" (284) and "BeautyBeam Carriers" (278). This indicates that these suppliers are frequently involved in issues that require resolution.
  • Flow Diagram Analysis: By examining the flow diagram, the Supply Chain Manager can trace specific issues back to these suppliers. For instance, if "GlamGlow Goods Delivery" is frequently associated with "Packaging Problems" or "Delivery Delays," these insights can be used to negotiate better terms or seek alternative suppliers.

Scenario 2: Product Quality Improvement

Persona: Product manager

Objective: Enhance product quality and customer satisfaction by addressing common product-related issues.

Actionable Insights:

  • Bar Chart Analysis: The "Nbr of Resolution per Product (Top 10)" chart highlights that "Skin Revive" and "Hair Revive" have the highest number of resolutions (48 and 41 respectively). This suggests that these products are frequently problematic.
  • Flow Diagram Analysis: The Product Manager can use the flow diagram to identify specific issues associated with these products. For example, "Skin Revive" might be linked to "Product Quality Issues" or "Application Difficulties."

Scenario 3: Customer Support Optimization

Persona: Customer support manager

Objective: Streamline customer support processes and improve resolution times.

Actionable Insights:

  • Bar Chart Analysis: The charts provide insights into which products and suppliers are generating the most issues, allowing the Customer Support Manager to allocate resources more effectively.
  • Flow Diagram Analysis: The flow diagram helps in understanding the complexity and interdependencies of various issues. For example, issues like "Instructions Clarity" and "Packaging Problems" might be common across multiple products and suppliers. GENAI can assist in automating responses to common issues, reducing the workload on customer support teams. Furthermore, by analyzing customer feedback, GENAI can provide insights into customer sentiment, helping the support team to prioritize issues that have the most significant impact on customer satisfaction.

    5. Interacting with GPT on Vectorised Documents

Various personas can utilize this interactive widget on the SAC dashboard to pose specific queries. These queries are converted into vector format, matched against Vectorised customer reviews stored in the SAP HANA database, and finally summarized by GPT. This approach facilitates a comprehensive understanding of customer sentiments, performance optimization, campaign effectiveness, product-specific feedback, and competitive analysis, ensuring data-driven strategies across various business functions.

Scenario 1: Customer Sentiment Analysis for Product Improvement

Persona: Product manager

Objective: Understand customer feedback and sentiment for product enhancement.

Actionable Insights:

  • Interactive Widget Analysis: The interactive widget in the fifth tab of the SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) dashboard allows the Product Manager to pose open-ended questions such as "Which is the best cream for young men?" Using GenAI, this query is translated into a vector format and matched against stored, vectorized customer reviews in the SAP HANA database. The result is a selection of relevant reviews and a summary generated by GPT, providing clear insights into customer preferences and sentiments. This enables the Product Manager to identify key areas for product improvement and innovation based on real customer feedback.

Scenario 2: Sales Performance Optimization

Persona: Sales manager

Objective: Optimize sales strategies and improve product performance.

Actionable Insights:

  • Interactive Widget Analysis: The sales manager can use the interactive widget to ask questions like "What are the common complaints about our top-selling products?" The Gen AI powered widget translates this query into a vector format and retrieves relevant customer reviews from the SAP HANA database. The GPT-generated summary provides a concise overview of common issues, allowing the sales manager to address these concerns proactively. This insight helps in refining sales strategies and improving customer satisfaction.

Scenario 3: Marketing Campaign Effectiveness

Persona: Marketing professional

Objective: Evaluate the effectiveness of marketing campaigns and understand customer sentiment.

Actionable Insights:

  • Interactive widget analysis: The Marketing Professional can utilize the interactive widget to ask questions such as "How do customers feel about our latest marketing campaign?" The Gen AI integration translates this query into a vector format and matches it with vectorized customer reviews in the SAP HANA database. The resulting summary generated by GPT provides a clear picture of customer sentiment towards the campaign. This insight helps the Marketing Professional to assess the campaign's impact and make data-driven decisions for future marketing strategies.

Scenario 4: Product-Specific Feedback Analysis

Persona: Quality assurance manager

Objective: Gather detailed feedback on specific products for quality improvement.

Actionable Insights:

  • Interactive Widget Analysis: The Quality assurance manager can use the interactive widget to ask specific questions like "What are the most common issues with our new skincare line?" The Gen AI powered widget translates this query into a vector format and retrieves relevant customer reviews from the SAP HANA database. The GPT-generated summary highlights the most common issues, providing the quality assurance manager with actionable insights to improve product quality and address customer concerns effectively.

Scenario 5: Competitive Analysis

Persona: Business analyst

Objective: Analyze competitor products and customer reviews for strategic planning.

Actionable Insights:

  • Interactive Widget Analysis: The business analyst can leverage the interactive widget to ask questions such as "How do our products compare to our competitors' in terms of customer satisfaction?" The Gen AI integration translates this query into a vector format and matches it with vectorized customer reviews from both the company's and competitors' products stored in the SAP HANA database. The GPT-generated summary provides a comparative analysis of customer satisfaction, helping the Business Analyst to identify strengths and weaknesses and develop strategic plans to gain a competitive edge.

    6. Customer 360

This tab showcases an Customer 360 Dashboard, integrating GenAI and analytics to provide a comprehensive view of customer interactions and sentiments. This powerful combination enables businesses to extract actionable insights, anticipate customer needs, and customize personalized experiences, driving increased customer satisfaction and loyalty.

Scenario 1: Customer Sentiment Analysis for Product Improvement

Persona: Product manager

Objective: Understand customer feedback and sentiment for product enhancement.

Scenario 2: Proactive Customer Support

Persona: Customer support agent

Objective: Provide personalized and informed customer support. The support agent uses the sentiment analysis and issue resolution data to reassure the customer about the price adjustment and highlight the product's value for money. For instance, if a customer contacts support with a query about the "BlendMaster" product, the agent can leverage the positive sentiment towards product quality and the resolved price issue to build trust and satisfaction.

Scenario 3: Targeted Marketing Campaigns

Persona: Marketing manager

Objective: Design effective marketing campaigns based on customer sentiment. Using the sentiment data, the marketing team identifies that customers appreciate the "TrimPro" product's reliability and wet trimming experience. The campaign can focus on these features to attract similar customer segments, leading to higher engagement and conversion rates.

Technical Coffee Corner

Let's explore the underlying architecture that empowers this sales strategy ecosystem. At the heart of this setup is the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine, which excels in handling high-dimensional vector data by creating a rich vector space representation for unstructured data like product reviews. When unified with the SAP Datasphere, this sophisticated engine facilitates efficient data ingestion, transformation, and harmonization, bridging the gap between isolated SAP S/4HANA transactional data and sentiment-driven insights. To enhance this further, SAP BTP plays a pivotal role in orchestrating these advanced analytics workflows, ensuring seamless data integration, real-time processing, and scalability. Leveraging the power of Large Language Models (LLM), the system employs fine-tuned AI models to contextualize and enrich product reviews, breaking them down into granular sentiment scores and categorized themes. These enriched insights are then fed into SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), where the robust analytics capabilities and interactive dashboards take center stage. SAC's connectivity and integration layers enable smooth data transfers, while its advanced algorithms enable predictive and prescriptive analytics, providing a multi-dimensional view of sales metrics. The cooperation of these platforms not only elevates the data processing capabilities but also equips businesses to generate forward-looking insights. For any help or clarification, you may contact us at @DKK  , @Dimitrios_Lyras .

Conclusion

Integrating Gen AI with SAP S/4HANA, SAP Datasphere, and SAP Analytics Cloud offers substantial benefits for both IT and business personas. For IT professionals, this combination simplifies the management of complex data landscapes, ensuring robust data security while enhancing the scalability of analytics tasks. With advanced analytical models in SAP Analytics Cloud, IT teams can more easily manage multiple data sources, thereby improving operational efficiency. Business users—including sales managers, marketing teams, product developers, and customer support agents—gain critical insights that drive strategic decision-making. The deep sentiment analysis enhances personalized marketing campaigns, improves customer support, and optimizes supply chain interactions. Utilizing these insights helps organizations shift from reactive to proactive strategies, significantly boosting sales performance, enhancing customer satisfaction, and increasing overall market responsiveness. This comprehensive approach ensures that both IT and business stakeholders can achieve their goals more effectively, leading to sustained business growth and a decisive competitive advantage.

entry.title: SAC Add-in for Powerpoint
entry.content:
Introduction

Hello and welcome to this blog discussing the powerful SAC Add-in tool developed by SAC to allow users to  integrate SAC with PowerPoint. In this blog I will discuss the following points:

  • How to get the add-in
  • What is the SAC add-in for PowerPoint?
  • Configurations and Set up

 

How to get the add-in

From the link below you can download and install the Microsoft add-in.

PowerPoint: https://appsource.microsoft.com/en-us/product/office/WA200007270?tab=Overview

 

What is the SAC add-in for PowerPoint?

The SAC add-in for Microsoft PowerPoint allows you to embed SAC widgets directly into your presentation slides. This ensures your slides are always up to date with accurate data from SAC, eliminating the need for manual updates. You can connect to an SAC tenant, select and filter widgets, and refresh data on demand, making your presentations dynamic and reliable.

Important things to note:

  • Table widgets cannot be used with this add-on
  • You can only use optimized stories with this add-on
  • The story must be saved to the Public folder
  • You can refresh the widget's data at any time on demand

 

Configurations and Set up

1. Open PowerPoint and, similar to the SAC add-in for Excel, you’ll be asked to enter your SAC URL

 

2. You’ll then be asked to login with your SAC tenant details. Once you log in you can see all your SAC tenants’ stories.

 

3. Select you story. In this example, we’ll use the SAP_SMPL_Planning story.

 

4. Once you click on your preferred story, you’ll get a list of the pages associated with your story.

5. You can then click on each page to see the available widgets from that SAC dashboard that can be used in PowerPoint.

 

6. You can then select the widget you want to insert and add it to your PowerPoint slide

 

7. Your slide should look like this once you’ve added your widget and combined other PowerPoint features

 

Conclusion

The SAC Add-in for PowerPoint provides an easy-to-use and powerful way to integrate SAC into your Microsoft Office workflow. By ensuring real-time data updates in PowerPoint presentations, this add-in empowers users to work more efficiently with the familiar Office interface. It helps users create dynamic presentations that support more informed and effective decision-making.

entry.title: Start Your scripting Journey The Easy Way With SAP Analytics Cloud | Part Three
entry.content:
The Journey

I've been using SAP Analytics Cloud for almost 8 years and like many of you, have stuck to stories rather than Analytic Applications as I don't have a massive coding background. Well if truth be told, I'm old and so my Basic and Pascal skills haven't had an airing for quite a few years.

With the latest innovations such as the Optimised Design Experience and the ability to add script elements to existing stories, I have now started to 'enhance' my visual storytelling. It struck me that rather than go big bang I would take an existing SAC Story and add code where I could get some quick wins without having to write big chunks of code. Links to Part One and Part Two of my series are included to make life easy for those of you who've not yet read them.

So for this instalment we have to thank a customer who recently asked me "how do I reset a story to its initial state using scripting?". That got me thinking because there is no script method to 'reset' a story to its initial state. There is no application.reset(); method so one will need to think a little outside the box.

Below is my take on a reset button that when pushed will reset the whole story to its initial state and if you've seen my blogs before you'll know I try to keep code to a minimum.

Let's start by creating an optimised story in SAC such as the one illustrated below. It's pretty simple with 1 chart and 3 filters but yours can be as complicated as you choose - it won't affect the outcome.

We are actually going to be use bookmarks to achieve the reset, but we need to do some prep before we can add the code. Once you story is complete, we'll need to set some preferences within the story itself. Start by selecting the Story Settings > View Time Settings > Bookmarks option as shown below:

This is where you define what objects within a story are saved when you add a bookmark to a specific story. For this to work we will need all objects, filters, charts etc to be saved with the bookmark so that when we apply it everything is returned to the initial state of the story. The dialog box opens, and you need to check the All Components in the Story option.

Save, close and again Save your story. We now have a completed story and next we need to save a bookmark for the initial state. For this we simply View or Present the story and from the toolbar add a bookmark (Bookmark Current State) with the following settings selected.

You must make sure you select all three checkboxes, give it a name and click Save. Once this is done you can apply that very same bookmark using the same menuitem to open the Initial State bookmark.

Now the clever part because once you apply that bookmark the browser URL will change and look something like the following:

https://SAP_Tenant/sap/fpa/ui/tenants/c40d8/app.html#/story2&/s2/25D3E6803716B26B8EA76751785064F0/?bookmarkId=22305278-9717-4296-8809-298841349359&mode=view

Final step is to copy the part of the URL that's shown above in Blue and Bold and keep it safe for the final step. Close the story view tab and edit the original story. You need to add a button and button script to make this button reset the story when pushed. I like to remove the button text, add a tooltip and add an icon to the button before adding the script... something like this...

and this...

All that remains is to add some script to that button and you done - you know me by now - it's one line of code. You will notice that the bookmark argument is the same text we saved above from the URL and this is what the apply() function expects.

 

// Click the button to apply the bookmark saved in the previous step

Bookmarks.apply('22305278-9717-4296-8809-298841349359');

 

We're all done, you can now save the story and use as you wish. You can play with the filters, edit them, drill the chart but then push the button and the story will be reset to its initial state. #simples.

entry.title: Translation of SAP Analytics Cloud content in multiple language Add-On
entry.content:
The objective from the customer was to support multiple language for reporting. Like in the example below:

Left : User with English language settings,  right: User with Japanese language settings.

 

We have to divide the translation process into four sections:

 

  • Model translation
  • Story translation
  • Allow translation
  • Dimension description translation, where possible.

Current help for translation: SAP Analytics Cloud - Translate 

 

Allow Translation

The configuration could be done at following location:
       Under the sidebar under "System/Administration" and the selected Tab the "System Configuration".

Toogle the switch "Allow translation of user content" to "on".

 

Model translation

In the model configuration, select "Model Preferences".

Under the language tab, click on the "Request Model Translation" button.

It will indicate with language has been started to be translated.

 

As a second under the sidebar "Translation" it will show the following entires:

The following objects will be created as entries dimensions and model. 

Selecting one of the entires, gives the user to option to enter manual for all the requested entries types.

Select target language first, after that enter missing entries.

This would be the manual translation.

The second option is to download the files to give to an external party or software to translate.

 

 

For each object one file would be created.

Below a detail breakdown of the file detail:

The file to upload has to follow the restrict configuration:

Filename has to be change to the following construct:
Source file name: Product.xlf. ----> Product_ja.xlf.   
Note: _ja indicates language to load, only one language per file possible!

If you are using a translation software like BTP service, where you can upload the extracted file directly,
It will return a file like below, which you have to modify.

example:

 

 

 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xliff xmlns="urn:oasis:names:tc:xliff:document:1.2" version="1.2">
    <file datatype="plaintext" original="self" source-language="en-US">
        <header>
            <sxmd:metadata xmlns:sxmd="urn:x-sap:mlt:xliff12:metadata:1.0" xmlns="urn:x-sap:mlt:tsmetadata:1.0">
                <object-name>t_2e3_2eCrok1j05m87bgsdpm71oja5q42_3AProduct</object-name>
            </sxmd:metadata>
        </header>
        <body>
            <group id="t.3.Crok1j05m87bgsdpm71oja5q42:Product">
                <trans-unit id="t.3.Crok1j05m87bgsdpm71oja5q42:Product_ProductCategory_Hier">
                    <source>Product Category</source>
                    <target>製品カテゴリ</target>
                </trans-unit>
                <trans-unit id="t.3.Crok1j05m87bgsdpm71oja5q42:Product_ID_Prop">
                    <source>Member ID</source>
                    <target>メンバー ID</target>
                </trans-unit>
            </group>
            <group id="artifactMetadata">
                <trans-unit id="t_2e3_2eCrok1j05m87bgsdpm71oja5q42_3AProduct_description">
                    <source>Product</source>
                    <target>製品</target>
                </trans-unit>
                <trans-unit id="t_2e3_2eCrok1j05m87bgsdpm71oja5q42_3AProduct_title">
                    <source>Product</source>
                    <target>製品</target>
                </trans-unit>
            </group>
        </body>
    </file>
</xliff>

 

 

 

 

Dimension description translation

Next item to change the dimension description, you have to go to dimension configuration inside the model.

Scroll down the properties to bottom, there you will find a toggle button to activate translation.

At top of the dimension member table, a button appears, which allow to switch between the languages. This way it allows you enter multiple description for different languages.

 

 

Story translation

The last one is the story translation, which could be activated by take the story into edit mode and clicking on "Story Settings and select "Story Details".

In the story details scroll down and look for the toggle "Enable Translation".


This will create entry again inside translation an entry.

Below the input screen with parameter for the a story example:

I hope this explains in more detail how to use the translation capabilities for SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: How Metadata Enrichment can enhance SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine on GenAI scenarios
entry.content:
Introduction

In the ever-evolving landscape of data-driven businesses, the need for intelligent data applications that can process and analyse vast amounts of data accurately and rapidly has never been more critical. There are already several interesting SAP Blogs regarding the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine and Langchain on our community page, such as  HANA Vector Engine and LangChain , SAP HANA Cloud's Vector Engine Announcement , Vectorize your Data : SAP HANA Cloud's Vector Engine for Unified Data Excellence , First steps using the Hana Vector Engine with SAP GEN AI  and more. On this blog we will focus on how Metadata Enrichment and the SAP HANA Vector Engine, can improve the performance of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG for short) scenarios.There are multiple ways by which we can leverage metadata enrichment into RAG scenarios, but for the purposes of the present case we will employ the OpenAi Metadata Tagger.

Why do I need Metadata Enrichment?

Metadata enrichment can significantly enhance Retriever-Augmented Generation (RAG) scenarios by providing additional, contextual information that helps in more accurate and relevant document retrieval. This information can include basic information such as the document title and page from which the information was retrieved but can also be extended to host even additional information, such as sentiment of the text chunk, topics discussed, summaries etc. By enhancing the documents with additional metadata the retriever's performance can be improved as these additional information can provide search handles and contextual cues that aid the retriever in finding more accurate matches from the corpus.

However, in real-life applications, several documents may need to be processed and undergoing the metadata enrichment task manually can be tedious and time consuming task. Nevertheless, by means of GenAI-based document transformers, we can streamline the metadata extraction process.

The benefits of Metadata Enrichments via a Business Example

In today's digital era, online platforms have become a prominent marketplace for a plethora of products, attracting millions of consumers worldwide. Consequently, these platforms are overwhelmed with customer reviews, which are pivotal for businesses to assess customer satisfaction and product performance.
However, manually analysing these reviews can be work-intensive and time-consuming. This is where sentiment analysis, comes into play.

Sentiment analysis, also known as opinion mining, refers to the use of text analysis, computational linguistics, and machine learning to identify and extract subjective information from source materials. In the context of online marketplaces, this means gauging customer sentiment towards products or services based on their reviews.

Business Cases for Sentiment Analysis of Customer Reviews

  1. Enhancing Customer Experience: The primary objective of any business is to meet and exceed customer expectations. By analysing customer sentiment, companies can gain insights into what customers like or dislike about their products or services. This knowledge can be leveraged to improve product offerings and enhance customer experience, leading to higher customer retention and increased sales.
